quiz_questions.question_text: In an experiment, the researcher:

options.text: Avoids collecting data
Only observes subjects
Deliberately imposes a treatment
Uses only surveys

quiz_questions.explanation: In an experiment, a treatment is deliberately applied to subjects to measure its effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: An observational study:

options.text: Is the same as a census
Manipulates variables
Collects data without imposing treatments
Always involves a placebo

quiz_questions.explanation: In an observational study, researchers observe and record data without manipulating any variables.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which study design is considered the gold standard for establishing causation?

options.text: A longitudinal observational cohort study
A cross-sectional voluntary response survey
A retrospective single-group case study
Randomized controlled double-blind experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: The randomized controlled double-blind experiment minimizes bias and confounding, making it the gold standard.

quiz_questions.question_text: The purpose of a control group is to:

options.text: Introduce bias
Observe only
Receive the highest dose
Provide a baseline for comparison

quiz_questions.explanation: The control group does not receive the treatment and serves as a baseline to compare against the treatment group.

quiz_questions.question_text: Selection bias occurs when:

options.text: The sample is perfectly random
Certain groups are more likely to be included in the study than others
The placebo works
The study has too many participants

quiz_questions.explanation: Selection bias means the sample is not representative because some groups are systematically over- or under-represented.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is randomization important in experiments?

options.text: It helps ensure that treatment and control groups are comparable
It increases bias
It is not important
It makes the study observational

quiz_questions.explanation: Randomization distributes potential confounders equally across groups, making them comparable.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which variable does the researcher manipulate in an experiment?

options.text: Lurking variable
Explanatory (independent) variable
Confounding variable
Response variable

quiz_questions.explanation: The explanatory or independent variable is the one deliberately manipulated by the researcher.

quiz_questions.question_text: Response bias can occur when:

options.text: Data is collected randomly
Participants give inaccurate answers due to social pressure
A census is conducted
The sample is too large

quiz_questions.explanation: Response bias happens when participants provide inaccurate or dishonest answers, often due to social desirability.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is NOT a type of bias?

options.text: Selection bias
Response bias
Measurement bias
Randomization bias

quiz_questions.explanation: Randomization is a tool used to reduce bias, not a type of bias itself.

quiz_questions.question_text: Blinding helps reduce:

options.text: Sample size
The number of variables
Bias from subjects' or researchers' expectations
The cost of the study

quiz_questions.explanation: Blinding prevents knowledge of group assignments from influencing results through expectations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a confounding variable?

options.text: A variable that is always controlled for in experiments
A variable that only appears in surveys
A variable that affects both the explanatory and response variables, making it hard to isolate cause and effect
The variable the researcher deliberately manipulates

quiz_questions.explanation: A confounding variable influences both the explanatory and response variables, creating a misleading association that makes it difficult to determine true cause and effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a placebo?

options.text: An inactive treatment given to the control group to mimic the real treatment
A real treatment with active ingredients
A method for selecting random samples
A type of sampling bias

quiz_questions.explanation: A placebo is an inactive treatment, such as a sugar pill, given to control group subjects so they cannot tell whether they received the real treatment.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'double-blind' mean in experimental design?

options.text: Only researchers are unaware of group assignments
Everyone knows the group assignments
Only subjects are unaware of group assignments
Neither subjects nor researchers know who is in the treatment or control group

quiz_questions.explanation: In a double-blind study, both the subjects and the researchers administering treatments are kept unaware of group assignments to prevent bias.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the placebo effect?

options.text: A type of random sampling method used to select participants from a population roster
A systematic statistical calculation error that inflates the significance of results
An improvement in patients who believe they are receiving treatment, even though they are not
A proven and verified medical treatment that has passed all clinical trial phases

quiz_questions.explanation: The placebo effect occurs when subjects experience real changes in symptoms simply because they believe they are being treated.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'bias' mean in a statistical study?

options.text: Random variation that occurs by chance
Using a control group in an experiment
Having a large sample size
A systematic error that consistently skews results in one direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Bias is a systematic error that consistently pushes study results away from the true value in a particular direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an explanatory variable?

options.text: The outcome measured in a study
A variable that is hidden from the researcher
A variable used only in surveys
The variable that is manipulated or thought to cause changes in another variable

quiz_questions.explanation: The explanatory (independent) variable is the factor the researcher manipulates or identifies as possibly causing changes in the response variable.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a response variable?

options.text: The outcome variable that is measured to see if the treatment had an effect
The variable the researcher deliberately changes
A variable that confounds the results
The answer a participant gives on a survey

quiz_questions.explanation: The response (dependent) variable is the outcome being measured in a study to determine whether the treatment had an effect.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'observer bias' mean?

options.text: Bias caused by the sample being too small
Bias caused by using a placebo
Bias that occurs when a researcher's expectations influence how they record or interpret data
Bias caused by random assignment

quiz_questions.explanation: Observer bias occurs when a researcher's knowledge of group assignments or expectations subtly influences their observations or data recording.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a lurking variable?

options.text: A hidden variable not included in the study that may affect the relationship between the variables being studied
A variable that is carefully measured in the study
The main variable the researcher manipulates
A variable that has been eliminated from the study

quiz_questions.explanation: A lurking variable is not measured or accounted for in the study but can influence the results and create misleading associations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'replication' mean in experimental design?

options.text: Repeating the same measurement on one subject
Copying someone else's study without permission
Publishing the same paper twice
Using enough subjects in each treatment group to detect meaningful differences

quiz_questions.explanation: Replication means having a sufficient number of subjects in each group so that results are reliable and differences can be detected with confidence.

quiz_questions.question_text: Researchers find that ice cream sales and drowning rates both rise in summer. The most likely confounding variable is:

options.text: There is no confounding variable
Ice cream causes drowning
Hot weather increases both ice cream sales and swimming activity
Drowning causes ice cream sales

quiz_questions.explanation: Hot weather is a confounding variable that drives both ice cream purchases and swimming (and thus drowning), creating a spurious correlation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher studying a new drug knows which patients received it and subtly treats them differently during evaluations. This is an example of:

options.text: Placebo effect
Selection bias
Confounding
Researcher bias (observer bias)

quiz_questions.explanation: When the researcher's knowledge of group assignments affects their behavior toward subjects, it introduces observer bias.

quiz_questions.question_text: A study tracks the eating habits and weight of 1,000 people over 5 years without imposing any dietary changes. This is best classified as:

options.text: A double-blind experiment
A randomized experiment
A census
An observational study

quiz_questions.explanation: Since no treatment is imposed and researchers simply observe and record, this is an observational study.

quiz_questions.question_text: A study finds that people who exercise more tend to be happier. Can we conclude that exercise causes happiness from this study alone?

options.text: Only if the sample is large enough
Not from an observational study alone; confounders may exist
Yes, the data clearly shows causation
Only if a graph is provided

quiz_questions.explanation: Observational studies show associations but cannot establish causation because possible confounding variables have not been controlled.

quiz_questions.question_text: An experiment without a control group is problematic because:

options.text: There is no baseline to compare the treatment's effect against
Control groups always add bias to results
It makes the sample larger than necessary
Control groups are too expensive to include

quiz_questions.explanation: Without a control group, researchers cannot determine whether the observed effect is due to the treatment or to other factors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company wants to test whether a new pain reliever works better than a sugar pill. They randomly assign 200 patients to receive either the drug or a placebo, and neither patients nor doctors know who gets which. This design is a:

options.text: Census
Single-blind observational study
Randomized double-blind placebo-controlled experiment
Convenience sample survey

quiz_questions.explanation: Random assignment, a placebo control, and double blinding make this a randomized double-blind placebo-controlled experiment.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher assigns the front row to use a new study method and the back row to use the old method, then compares test scores. What is the main flaw?

options.text: The teacher used a placebo
There is no randomization, so seating position could confound the results
The study is double-blind
The sample size is too large

quiz_questions.explanation: Without random assignment, students in the front row may differ systematically from those in the back, making seating position a confounding variable.

quiz_questions.question_text: A survey is posted on a website asking visitors to rate a new product. Only people with very strong opinions bother to respond. This results in:

options.text: Voluntary response bias
A well-designed experiment
Observer bias
A perfectly random sample

quiz_questions.explanation: Voluntary response surveys over-represent people with strong opinions, creating a biased and unrepresentative sample.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospital tests two surgical procedures by letting surgeons choose which one to use on each patient. Surgeons tend to use the newer procedure on healthier patients. When comparing outcomes, the newer procedure appears better. The problem is:

options.text: Blinding was used when it shouldn't have been
Patient health is a confounding variable because assignment was not random
The older procedure is always worse
The hospital used too many patients

quiz_questions.explanation: Because healthier patients were systematically assigned to the newer procedure, patient health confounds the comparison, and we cannot tell if the procedure or the health status caused better outcomes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school wants to test whether free breakfast improves attendance. They randomly select 5 of their 10 elementary schools to offer free breakfast; the other 5 serve as controls. After one semester, they compare average attendance rates. Which key principle of experimental design does this scenario demonstrate?

options.text: Observational design, because no treatment was imposed
Random assignment with a control group to establish a causal comparison
Blinding, because students don't know they are in a study
Convenience sampling, because the schools were easy to access

quiz_questions.explanation: Randomly assigning schools to treatment (free breakfast) and control groups allows the school district to compare outcomes and draw causal conclusions about breakfast's effect on attendance.
